If a warranty claim is submitted without one-time use parts required to properly repair a customer vehicle, the warranty claim may be subject to denial or chargeback.
dealer_actions: Generate a repair order (written or computer generated) when an inspection, repair, replacement, adjustment, Pre-Delivery Inspection, documentation of a customer statement, or campaign repair is required.
Do not always generate a repair order for requests indicating only the need for an explanation of a feature or operation of a component/accessory.
Check the Dealership’s customer service file and the Hyundai WebDCS Warranty Vehicle Information screen during the repair order write-up process.
Attach a printout of the Warranty Vehicle Information screen to the service file copy of the repair order, including for electronic service files.
Ensure the repair order form utilized is clear and legible, contains specific details of information, and is administered according to HMA stipulations.

Ensure the repair order form utilized for warranty claim submission contains at least four copies: Dealer Accounting File Copy, Service File Copy or Parts Scrapping Copy, Shop Hard Copy (Technician Input), and Customer Receipt Copy.
Ensure carbonless or transferable areas on the repair order form facilitate the transfer of appropriate information to certain copies only.
Retain the repair order copies in the appropriate area.
Keep Accounting copy files in numerical sequence in the business office.
Retain Customer service file copies and technician input shop hard copies in the service department, organized by vehicle identification number (VIN) in numerical sequence.
If using an electronic repair order system, retain copies of the initial customer write-up repair order with accounting files along with a copy of the final invoice.
If using an electronic repair order system, retain electronic facsimile paper copies of the final repair order in the customer service file in the service department.
Provide each owner for whom warranty or customer satisfaction service is performed with a completed copy of the repair order stating all repairs and services performed.
If using an electronic repair order system, provide the customer with a copy of both the original initial write-up repair order and the final invoice.

If using an electronic repair order system, retain Customer service file copies of both the original initial write-up repair order and the final invoice, including all parts replaced, in the service department, organized by VIN in numerical sequence.

For Pre-Delivery Inspection repair orders, include the Service Manager’s clear and legible signature, Dealer Logo and Address, Repair Order Number, Vehicle Identification Number (VIN), vehicle mileage before and after the required test drive, Technician time recording (refer to section 6.3), Repair Order Date, and Purchaser’s name, address and telephone number (or dealer’s assigned vehicle stock number if not sold).

For warranty, Campaign, or Goodwill repair orders, include Repair Order Number, Dealer Logo and Address, Vehicle Identification Number (VIN), Vehicle Mileage (In and Out), Repair Order Open Date and Close Date, Vehicle Delivery Date or Date of First Use, Owner/Customer’s name, address and telephone number, Complaint, Cause, Correction, Customer’s signature for initial repair order write-up and for receipt of vehicle, Claim Number(s), Prior approval number (if applicable), Nature Code and Explanation, Cause Code and Explanation, Labor Dollar Amount (if applicable), Labor Operation Code(s), Quantities and Hours (if applicable), Part(s) Dollar Amount (if applicable), Part(s) Quantity (if applicable), Causal Part Number, Replacement Part Numbers (if applicable), Sublet Amount and Sublet Code(s) (if applicable), Sublet Final Invoice Number and Name (if applicable), Towing Amount (if applicable), Towing Invoice Number and Name (if applicable), Freight Amount (if applicable), Freight Carrier (if applicable), Freight Shipping Date (if applicable), For Replacement Service Parts: the date of previous, original installation; previous, original mileage, and previous, original repair order or counter sales ticket number (if applicable), and Technician Comments.

When a new speedometer is installed due to failure, recalibrate it with the vehicle mileage at the time of failure replacement; if vendor for recalibration is unavailable, adjust new odometer to 0 miles and affix a Speedometer/Odometer Repair/Replacement Sticker (part #00924-00000) to the driver side door frame.
If a vehicle with a Speedometer/Odometer Repair/Replacement Sticker comes in for service, add the mileages of the odometer and sticker to obtain the true mileage.
Separate the technician input/shop hard copy from the repair order’s remaining soft copies prior to the work being performed.
Give the technician the technician input/shop hard copy of the repair order as their work copy at the time of repair performance.
Send the remaining repair order soft copies to the Parts Department or elsewhere as needed.
Send the complete repair order, including the technician input/shop hard copy and all remaining soft copies, to the appropriate area for close out after completion of the repair.

Ensure the repair order contains all parts replaced as per the repair, including complete Replacement Part Number(s), Replacement Part Quantity(s) issued, and Dealer Net cost of replacement part(s) on the date the repair is performed (with extended total for quantities greater than 1).

If an automated parts invoice or separate parts invoice is used, ensure it contains a cross-reference to the repair order and is attached to the technician input/shop hard copy retained in the customer/VIN service file, and ensure the repair order cross-references the parts invoice.
Obtain the customer's signature and approval for all repairs stated on the repair order, both prior to the work being performed and upon receipt of the vehicle after completion of the repairs.
If utilizing an electronic repair order system, ensure a customer signature is obtained on both the original initial write-up repair order copy and the final invoice copy.

Accept digital signatures or other electronic acknowledgements (e.g., signatures on a tablet, approval via text, email, or other app) for capturing customer authorizations, ensuring they are dated and time stamped, printed, attached, and retained as part of the work order.

If the customer uses a night drop envelope, ensure it contains the customer’s signature and date of drop off, and retain the signed night drop envelope in the service files attached to the technician input/shop hard copy of the repair order.
In the event a customer signature cannot be obtained on a night drop envelope of a repair order, the Service Manager must sign and date the repair order.
The Service Manager must sign the repair order for vehicles repaired that are in stock and for repair orders issued to perform the Pre-Delivery Inspection.
Accept digital signatures (including those captured via tablet) for the Service Manager’s signature.
Upon completion of repairs and receipt of the vehicle, provide the customer with the customer receipt copy of the completed repair order, stating all warranty repairs performed and parts replaced.

Ensure the technician input/shop hard copy or electronic hard copy of the repair order contains the technician’s comments and description of the repair performed, including the cause of the customer’s complaint, notes describing the diagnostic process, and the corrective action used to resolve the complaint.
Record and document technical measurements or readings required by applicable shop manuals on the technician input shop hard copy/electronic hard copy of the repair order.
Ensure repairs follow shop manuals procedures, including replacing one-time use parts.
Charge out one-time use parts designated to perform the repair on the warranty repair order to properly complete the repair.
Ensure all repairs are carried out in accordance with the latest published procedures detailed in shop manuals or other service-related publications such as Technical Service Bulletins and campaigns.
Refer to the most recent model and model year specific repair manual Technical Service Bulletin for diagnostic and available repair details.

Record and document before and after readings/measurements for parts requiring them, including but not limited to: Air conditioning Evac & Recharge refrigerant, Automatic transmission oil pressure test/Diagnosis Worksheet, ATM Tester, Battery tester readings/Battery code and printout, Brake discs/rotors, Brake shoes, Idle speed/Throttle body, Cylinder compression, Cylinder leak down, Emission test readings, Engine Diagnosis Worksheet, GDS test printouts/readings, Gear box preload, Headlamp aiming, Ignition timing, Oil consumption test, Test drive results, Valve clearances, Vehicle Tracking Data Sheet, Wheel alignment (including Tire Balance Data Sheet), and Wheel balance.
If utilizing an electronic repair order, ensure technicians input their comments into a computer terminal.

Generate and retain a hard copy of the repair order with the technician’s comments in the customer/VIN service file whenever technician’s comments are updated (also applies to repairs orders when information is entered using a digital service lane technology application).

Identify the individual technician performing the repair by name or number on the face of the technician input shop hard copy of the repair order next to each labor operation performed, ensuring identification is true and accurate (applies to team systems; team identification alone is not adequate; a code or name must also be utilized with an electronic repair order system).

Transfer all information added to the technician input shop hard copy of the repair order after the initial repair order write-up with the customer to the remaining repair order soft copies, including the customer receipt copy.

If using an electronic repair order system, print and retain subsequent generations of a given repair order in the customer/VIN service file such that the evolution of a repair order can be demonstrated.

For add-on warranty repairs made without a customer complaint: the technician must provide a complete description of the needed work on the hard copy of the repair order, including cause (with diagnosis notes) and corrective action.

For add-on warranty repairs made without a customer complaint: the Service Manager must verify the needed repair and sign next to the added repair on the hard copy of the repair order (not at the top or bottom of the invoice) prior to the work being performed.

For add-on warranty repairs made without a customer complaint: if the customer was notified by phone, document the date, time, contact method, and name of the customer who authorized the add-on repair on the hard copy of the repair order (the authorizing customer should be the same as on the repair order).
For add-on warranty repairs made without a customer complaint: transfer the additional repair information to all remaining copies of the repair order.

For add-on warranty repairs made without a customer complaint: the customer’s signature must appear next to the additional repair that has been added to the repair order (not at the top or bottom of the invoice).
For add-on warranty repairs made without a customer complaint with an electronic repair order system: indicate the add-on repair with an identifier such as an “*”.

If a warranty repair is generated from a customer pay repair order (where separate ROs are used for customer pay and warranty), ensure the customer pay repair order has the Service Manager’s signature next to the added repair(s) on the hard copy, the warranty repair description, and a cross-reference to the warranty repair order.
For customer add-on repairs (complaints added by customer after initial write-up), document the date and time the additional request was made on the repair order.
For customer add-on repairs: the Service Manager must sign next to the added customer complaint before the repair work is performed (not at the top or bottom of the invoice).

For customer add-on repairs: if the customer was notified by phone, document the date, time, contact method, and name of the customer who authorized the add-on repair on the hard copy of the repair order (the authorizing customer should be the same as on the repair order).
When a repair cannot be completed due to replacement parts not being available, do not submit the warranty claim for diagnosis or repair until the part is installed and the repair completed.
Return drivable vehicles to the customer while waiting for parts to arrive for incomplete repairs.
Accurately fill in the “date/mileage in” and “date/mileage out” fields on the repair order when the vehicle is returned to the customer while waiting for parts.
Add a statement to the repair order indicating that the vehicle is drivable and was returned while waiting for parts to arrive.
When a part for an incomplete repair arrives, open a new repair order using the date and mileage of when the vehicle was originally presented for diagnosis or repair.
On the new repair order for an incomplete repair, refer to the original repair order number when the part was originally ordered and repeat the original customer complaint statement.
In the customer concern section of the new repair order for an incomplete repair, input the repair completion date and mileage.

In the customer concern section of the new repair order for an incomplete repair, include: the date and mileage when the vehicle was originally presented for diagnosis or repair; the date the parts were ordered; the parts status at the time originally ordered (e.g., backordered); the date the parts arrived at the dealer; and the date and mileage of when the repairs were completed.

title: Digital Documentation Requirements for Warranty, Campaign, and Goodwill Repairs
raw_content: Digital documentation of warranty, campaign, and goodwill repairs requires video or photo attachments of VIN, Miles, Part and Sublet(when applicable) to the claim. Dealer can use GDS/STUI or any electronic device to capture the required repair information, photos or videos, to attach to the claim. Using GDS/STUI can automatically capture vehicle’s VIN and Miles (2019MY+ only, for older models take photo of odometer), however, a photo must be uploaded to the Submission Process Tracker in GDS for the auto capture info to transfer to HMA. Photos and videos uploaded to HMA via STUI are available in WebDCS to attach to the claim. Photos, videos, and documents captured by the technician related to the repair must be attached to the claim in WebDCS. The photos and/or videos should be digital, colored, high- resolution, original, and unedited displaying the defective part and/or the area of concern. A category must be assigned to each attachment file. In cases where GDS/STUI is not available, such as an outage, dealer may take photos or videos using separate device and manually attach files to the claim. Multiple uses of the same photo image for Prior Approval submission is not allowed. If a warranty, campaign, or goodwill repair attaches the required digital documentation to substantiate the claim at submission, HMA will not require separate and distinguishable technician on/off punch times for any job that is identified through a labor operation in the Labor Time Standard, as well as any Campaign/Recall or op code in a TSB. It is the responsibility of the Dealer to verify that the required digital documentation is attached to the claim at the time the claim is submitted for payment. Straight Time (NTT, ATT), and claims (including Campaign/Recall) utilizing non-HMA LTS hours must still be supported by separate and distinguishable on/off punch times, see sections 5.8 and 6.3. Note: Pre-Delivery Inspection (PDI) claims do not require technician punch times. For campaigns/recalls, please check the TSB instructions for specific STUI/digital documentation requirements. If not specified in TSB, then VIN and Miles are required. Document the repair or part replacement as instructed in the TSB. HMA reserves the right at any time in its sole discretion to require a dealer to submit punch times (see sections 5.8 and 6.3) instead of the digital documentation policy outlined above. Record Retention & Administration Requirements 141
coverage_details: Digital documentation for warranty, campaign, and goodwill repairs
Use of GDS/STUI or any electronic device to capture required repair information
Automatic capture of VIN and Miles for 2019MY+ vehicles via GDS/STUI
Photos and videos uploaded to HMA via STUI are available in WebDCS for attachment

Waiver of separate and distinguishable technician on/off punch times for jobs identified through Labor Time Standard labor operations, Campaign/Recall, or TSB op codes, when required digital documentation is attached
exclusions: Multiple uses of the same photo image for Prior Approval submission
Straight Time (NTT, ATT) and claims (including Campaign/Recall) utilizing non-HMA LTS hours, which still require separate and distinguishable on/off punch times
Pre-Delivery Inspection (PDI) claims do not require technician punch times
HMA reserves the right to require submission of punch times instead of digital documentation policy
dealer_actions: Attach video or photo attachments of VIN, Miles, Part, and Sublet (when applicable) to the claim
Use GDS/STUI or any electronic device to capture required repair information, photos, or videos
For older models (pre-2019MY), take a photo of the odometer for miles
Upload a photo to the Submission Process Tracker in GDS for auto-captured VIN and Miles to transfer to HMA
Attach photos, videos, and documents captured by the technician related to the repair to the claim in WebDCS
Ensure photos and/or videos are digital, colored, high-resolution, original, and unedited, displaying the defective part and/or area of concern
Assign a category to each attachment file
In cases where GDS/STUI is unavailable (e.g., outage), take photos or videos using a separate device and manually attach files to the claim
Verify that the required digital documentation is attached to the claim at the time of submission for payment
Check TSB instructions for specific STUI/digital documentation requirements for campaigns/recalls
If not specified in TSB for campaigns/recalls, VIN and Miles are required digital documentation
Document the repair or part replacement as instructed in the TSB

title: GDS Basic Connection (BASICA00) Claim Submission
raw_content: Claim submission for GDS Basic Connection of operation code BASICA00 (0.1) applies to warranty, paint, anti-perforation, hybrid, emissions, and powertrain claim types. BASICA00 does not apply to other claims, such as campaign, retail part, or goodwill claim types. BASICA00 also does not apply without a related warranty repair claim. BASICA00 can only be submitted once per repair order. If the repair order contains a diagnostic RQ0 (0.3) operation code or No Trouble Found (NTF), BASICA00 will not apply for claim submission. All modules must be selected from the GDS Fault Code Searching (FCS) and the GDS results must be uploaded to HMA in order to qualify for BASICA00 submission. As mentioned in 6.2.1 Repair Order Retention Requirements, a GDS screenshot should be saved in the repair order documentation retention and the screenshot should be attached onto a claim if FCS GDS results cannot be uploaded to HMA. Record Retention & Administration Requirements 142
coverage_details: Applies to warranty claim types
Applies to paint claim types
Applies to anti-perforation claim types
Applies to hybrid claim types
Applies to emissions claim types
Applies to powertrain claim types
exclusions: Does not apply to campaign claim types
Does not apply to retail part claim types
Does not apply to goodwill claim types
Does not apply without a related warranty repair claim
Can only be submitted once per repair order
Does not apply if the repair order contains a diagnostic RQ0 (0.3) operation code
Does not apply if the repair order indicates No Trouble Found (NTF)
dealer_actions: All modules must be selected from the GDS Fault Code Searching (FCS)
GDS results must be uploaded to HMA to qualify for BASICA00 submission
A GDS screenshot should be saved in the repair order documentation retention
A GDS screenshot should be attached to a claim if FCS GDS results cannot be uploaded to HMA

title: Record Retention & Administration Requirements
raw_content: As stated in the Sales and Service Agreement, Dealers are required to maintain all warranty and policy repair orders and related documentation for a period of five years, exclusive of any retention period required by any governmental entity. The retention period begins with the date of claim payment, not from the date the repair was performed. Dealers must retain all substantiating records and documents for all claims submitted to HMA for payment. The supporting documents must be arranged in a systematic order and be readily accessible for HMA review. Note: Repair order copies requested by HMA must be submitted to HMA by the Dealer within ten business days from the date of the request. The following list of supporting records and documents must be available for examination by HMA’s representatives: • Technician input/shop hard copy, service file copy (both original initial write-up repair order and final invoice copies for electronic systems) and parts scrapping copy of the repair order. • Dealer accounting file copy of the repair order (This copy must list the part(s) replaced and must indicate the labor charge). • The DCS acknowledgment (Claim Status Report) copy (from the day after submission) and any supporting documents, properly cross-referenced, must be retained when a warranty claim is subsequently prepared and submitted to HMA. • Attached to the 10 year/100,000 mile Powertrain Repair Order and retained in the dealer files: A fully executed 10 year/100,000 mile Powertrain Limited Warranty Original Owner Verification Affidavit Form, along with the documents listed below. 1. A photocopy of the customer’s current and up-to-date (not expired) owner registration from Department of Motor Vehicles (DMV) as required by Prior Approval Request Submission (5.4.2) for 10/100k Powertrain warranty validation. 2. A printout of the Warranty Vehicle Information Screen. • All detailed records used to prepare payroll for Dealer employees who performed the particular repairs or replacements (includes time ticket and flag sheet hard copies). • Original or true copies of final invoices covering parts or services purchased outside, and the record of payment of such invoices, (e.g. sublet repair final invoices, purchase orders, accounts payable files, parts inventory files, open account statements, etc.) along with Rental Agreements and other relevant documents • New vehicle invoices • Customer sales files (includes copy of Retail Delivery Registration (RDR) form, customer sales contract, etc.) Record Retention & Administration Requirements 143 • Customer service files (includes Pre-Delivery Checklist, Vehicle Delivery Presentation, and copies of all repair orders including warranty, service campaign, Dealer internals, customer pay, body shop estimates, in-transit damage, etc.) • Warranty Claim Register • Dealer Financial Statements • Loading/Distribution Sheets • GDS machine before and after readings – upon completion of any test, take screenshot using GDS screen capture function for attachment to claim • Any other records used by the Dealer to support the information recorded on the claim form These requirements fully apply to electronic repair order systems as well as traditional manual repair order generation systems.

title: Record Retention and Administration Requirements
raw_content: Hyundai supports the use of fully integrated document retention systems that meet or exceed the following requirements. All documentation must: • Be complete and retained as outlined in 6.2.1 Repair Order Retention Requirements in effect at the time of the repair. • Be clearly legible for review and available upon request. • Be easily retrievable by VIN sequence. • Be backed up to a separate storage drive, providing redundancy for all customer and vehicle data. Original documents may be discarded only if there is a way to retrieve acceptable electronically stored documents that are identical copies of the original, and the dealer must have a back-up recovery plan to replace electronically stored documents in the event of disaster. Document storage systems must: • Prohibit changes to the document after it has been imaged. If modification is necessary, both original and modified copies must be available, with complete explanation to include the name of the individual who approved the RO modification and date of modification. • Protects against information being altered or destroyed once a repair order is finally closed. Note: Any electronic communication (E.g., text messages, email, social media posts) done between the dealer and customer regarding any form of repair approval or update must be time stamped and stored in a manner that cannot be altered and can be retrieved in case of future audit or other business needs of HMA. Digital signatures, or other electronic acknowledgements Record Retention & Administration Requirements 144 (E.g., signatures on a tablet, approval via text/email, or other apps), are acceptable means of capturing customer authorizations and must also be dated and stored in a manner that cannot be altered and can be retrieved in case of future audit or other business need of HMA. In the event that a dealer is unable to produce sufficient RO documentation upon request or required control features are not being utilized, HMA reserves the right to chargeback the subject claims. Dealerships may utilize an electronic document storage system that meets the above requirements to retain all required documentation for the 5 year total retention period as required per the HMA Dealer Sales and Service Agreement. In certain states or local jurisdictions, additional requirements may be stipulated. Dealers should contact their legal counsel for clarification on this issue. Record Retention & Administration Requirements 145
coverage_details: Use of fully integrated document retention systems that meet or exceed specified requirements is supported.
Original documents may be discarded if identical electronic copies are retrievable and a backup recovery plan exists.
Digital signatures and electronic acknowledgements (e.g., signatures on tablets, approvals via text/email) are acceptable for customer authorizations.
Electronic document storage systems meeting requirements are permitted for the 5-year retention period as per HMA Dealer Sales and Service Agreement.
exclusions: Discarding original documents without identical, retrievable electronic copies and a backup recovery plan.
Modification of imaged documents without retaining both original and modified copies, along with a complete explanation including approver name and date.
Inability to produce sufficient Repair Order (RO) documentation upon request.
Failure to utilize required control features in document retention systems.
Claims subject to chargeback if RO documentation is insufficient or control features are not utilized.
dealer_actions: Ensure all documentation is complete and retained as outlined in section 6.2.1 Repair Order Retention Requirements.
Ensure all documentation is clearly legible for review and available upon request.
Ensure all documentation is easily retrievable by VIN sequence.
Back up all customer and vehicle data to a separate storage drive for redundancy.
Have a back-up recovery plan to replace electronically stored documents in the event of disaster.
Ensure document storage systems prohibit changes to a document after it has been imaged.

If a document modification is necessary, retain both original and modified copies with a complete explanation, including the name of the individual who approved the RO modification and the date of modification.
Ensure document storage systems protect against information being altered or destroyed once a repair order is finally closed.

Ensure all electronic communication (e.g., text messages, email, social media posts) regarding repair approval or updates are time-stamped and stored in an unalterable and retrievable manner for audit/business needs.
Ensure all digital signatures and electronic acknowledgements are dated and stored in an unalterable and retrievable manner for audit/business needs.
Retain all required documentation for a 5-year total retention period.
Contact legal counsel for clarification on additional requirements stipulated by certain states or local jurisdictions.

title: Record Retention & Administration Requirements
raw_content: The actual starting and ending times for all warranty repairs must be documented using one of these methods: • A time punch clock which must show month, day, and time. • An electronic repair order (ERO) system that records the times automatically on the service file or invoice copy of the repair order. If a time punch clock is used, technicians must record their actual beginning and ending times by punching on and off of a Daily Time Ticket (flag sheet). Customer pay and internal work performed on the same repair order with warranty work requires separate time recording. The information on each time flag must be completely filled out. This includes the repair order number, technician identification, the type of repair performed (customer pay, internal, or warranty), labor time standard hours, and the appropriate line on the repair order that corresponds with the repair performed. The time flag adhesive strips must be removed from the flag sheet and affixed to the back of the repair order hard copy. The carbon copy of the flag sheet must be retained by the dealership for a period of five years, exclusive of any retention period required by any governmental entity. HMA reserves the right to chargeback any claim where the Dealer failed to retain the carbon copy of the flag sheet. HMA will accept computerized clocking entries from an electronic repair order system provided they reflect “ON” and “OFF” entries. The actual system generated technician time tracking must be printed, and appear on the service file or invoice copy of the repair order. Time punch clocks are required when utilizing an electronic repair order system that does not generate “ON” and “OFF” clocking substantiation. Actual labor time operations (ATT and NTT) not listed in the Labor Time Standard Manual that are listed on claims submitted to HMA for reimbursement must comply with the following requirements: • The technician must provide a detailed explanation of why actual time is needed on the hard copy of the repair order. Technician comments should be entered in the claim in the ‘Additional comments’ field. If Techline was contacted, the Techline Case # should also be included. • Service Manager must review all actual time diagnosis and repairs. The Service Manager must consent to the actual time next to the repair line by: • Signing his or her name • Printing his or her name • Noting the time consented A Service Manager’s signature at the top or bottom of the invoice is not acceptable. Record Retention & Administration Requirements 146 • A separate time recording is required to document the actual time spent by the technician. Each actual time operation requires separate beginning and ending time recording. • For diagnostic actual time labor operations, an explanation of the applicable diagnostic equipment and method must be documented on the hard copy of the repair order. The documentation must also include before and after readings/measurements. The general requirements shown below are to be followed for all time operations by all Dealers on claims submitted to HMA. • The technician’s time may not be recorded on two or more repairs at the same time. Overlapping time recorded on two or more repairs will be subject to chargeback. To avoid the possibility of overlapping time recording, no more than one repair order may be assigned to a technician at any given time. • If the technician inadvertently forgets to punch the beginning or ending time for a given repair, the Service Manager may handwrite the missing time punch. The Service Manager must authorize the time recording by recording the date and signing next to the flag strip affixed to the hard copy of the repair order. • Only authorized members of service management who are non-production/working technicians may enter handwritten time. A member of service management who also performs warranty repairs and is separately paid for such work is not authorized to sign time clocking. In this case, the Dealership General Manager must sign the handwritten time and actual time repairs. • When more than one technician performs repairs on the same vehicle, the time for each technician (including team lead technicians) must be recorded separately. The time flag for each technician must be affixed to the back of the repair order hard copy. • Starting and ending times for PDI repair orders must be accounted for separately from time spent installing accessories or completing other repairs. The Service Manager must authorize any time spent on repairs for vehicles during the PDI process by signing next to the explanation of why the additional work is required. • When a repair is not completed on the same day, the technician is taken off the job, or when the technician takes a lunch or personal break, the time off and the time resumed must be documented. All time recording must reflect the actual starting and ending times for the repair. NOTE: Digital documented warranty, campaign, and goodwill repairs do not require technician to punch on and off any job that is identified through a labor operation in the Labor Time Standard, as well as any Campaign/Recall or op code in a TSB. Straight Time (NTT, ATT), and claims (including Campaign/Recall) utilizing non-HMA LTS hours must still be supported by separate and distinguishable on/off punch times, see section 6.1.1. Record Retention & Administration Requirements 147
coverage_details: Actual starting and ending times for all warranty repairs must be documented using a time punch clock showing month, day, and time, or an electronic repair order (ERO) system that automatically records times.

For diagnostic actual time labor operations, document an explanation of the applicable diagnostic equipment and method, including before and after readings/measurements, on the hard copy of the repair order.
Ensure the technician’s time is not recorded on two or more repairs at the same time.
Assign no more than one repair order to a technician at any given time.
If a technician inadvertently forgets to punch the beginning or ending time, the Service Manager may handwrite the missing time punch.
Service Manager must authorize handwritten time recording by recording the date and signing next to the flag strip affixed to the hard copy of the repair order.
Ensure only authorized members of service management who are non-production/working technicians enter handwritten time.
If a member of service management performs warranty repairs and is separately paid, the Dealership General Manager must sign the handwritten time and actual time repairs.
When more than one technician performs repairs on the same vehicle, record the time for each technician separately.
Affix the time flag for each technician to the back of the repair order hard copy.
Account for starting and ending times for PDI repair orders separately from time spent installing accessories or completing other repairs.
Service Manager must authorize any time spent on repairs for vehicles during the PDI process by signing next to the explanation of why the additional work is required.
Document time off and time resumed when a repair is not completed on the same day, the technician is taken off the job, or when the technician takes a lunch or personal break.
Ensure all time recording reflects the actual starting and ending times for the repair.
Provide separate and distinguishable on/off punch times for Straight Time (NTT, ATT) and claims (including Campaign/Recall) utilizing non-HMA LTS hours.

title: Time Recording Requirements and Payroll Documentation
raw_content: Time recording requirements and payroll documentation apply to all technicians– whether working individually or working as part of a team. The additional time recording requirements as they apply to technician team service operations are: • Each technician’s time (including the lead technician’s time) must be recorded separately by clocking on and off of a daily time ticket (flag sheet). • The information on each time flag must be completely filled out. This includes the repair order number, technician identification, the type of repair performed (customer pay, internal, or warranty) labor time hours, and the appropriate line on the repair order that corresponds with the repair performed. • The hard copy of the repair order must contain the name or number of the technician(s) who performed the repair alongside the description of the repair. Team only identification is not acceptable. • The Labor Time Standard applies regardless of technician pay plans or working arrangements. For example, a trainee technician may remove and reinstall a transmission while the actual repair is performed by an expert technician. Reimbursement is based on the applicable labor time standard operations regardless of the fact that two persons work on the repair at the same time or that the actual time required for the repair may be in excess of the labor time standard because of the use of a trainee technician. NOTE: Digital documented warranty, campaign, and goodwill repairs do not require technician to punch on and off any job that is identified through a labor operation in the Labor Time Standard, as well as any Campaign/Recall or op code in a TSB. Straight Time (NTT, ATT), and claims (including Campaign/Recall) utilizing non-HMA LTS hours must still be supported by separate and distinguishable on/off punch times, see section 6.1.1. Record Retention & Administration Requirements 148
coverage_details: Time recording requirements and payroll documentation apply to all technicians, whether working individually or as part of a team.
Reimbursement for repairs is based on the applicable labor time standard operations, regardless of technician pay plans or working arrangements.
The Labor Time Standard applies even if two persons work on the repair at the same time or if the actual time required exceeds the standard due to the use of a trainee technician.
exclusions: Digital documented warranty, campaign, and goodwill repairs do not require technicians to punch on and off for any job identified through a labor operation in the Labor Time Standard, or any Campaign/Recall or op code in a TSB.
dealer_actions: Each technician’s time (including the lead technician’s time) must be recorded separately by clocking on and off of a daily time ticket (flag sheet).

Each time flag must be completely filled out with the repair order number, technician identification, type of repair performed (customer pay, internal, or warranty), labor time hours, and the appropriate line on the repair order.
The hard copy of the repair order must contain the name or number of the technician(s) who performed the repair alongside the description of the repair.
Team only identification for technicians on repair orders is not acceptable.
Straight Time (NTT, ATT), and claims (including Campaign/Recall) utilizing non-HMA LTS hours must still be supported by separate and distinguishable on/off punch times.

title: Record Retention & Administration Requirements
raw_content: Documentation must be provided to substantiate that a specific part stated on the warranty claim was used to repair the vehicle. The repair order must indicate and include the parts charged out to the repair order for a specific vehicle. For Dealers using an automated parts inventory system, a numbered counter requisition cross- referenced with the repair order or point of sale document and a date of transfer of parts from inventory, must be completed each time parts are removed from inventory. Each counter requisition number must be recorded on the repair order or the point of sale document. Every part removed from inventory must be listed on a counter requisition. Counter requisitions must be accounted for and filed in numeric sequence. All invoices or copies covering parts or services purchased from vendors or other shops (and a record of payment of such invoices), new car invoices and any other records used by the Dealer to substantiate the information recorded on the claim, must be retained by the Dealer for a period of five (5) years from date of claim payment. Repairs performed using other than Genuine Hyundai Parts purchased from HMA must have the non-genuine Hyundai part supplier’s name, invoice number, and part number recorded on all copies of the repair order including the customer’s receipt copy. The supplier’s invoice must be retained with other accounting files. The use of non-genuine Hyundai parts for warranty repairs requires prior approval from the District Parts and Service Manager. Non-genuine Hyundai Parts include Hyundai-branded parts that are not supplied by HMA and are intended for use outside the U.S. In the event that a requisitioned part is not utilized in a repair and returned to inventory, this fact must be documented. Dealers who utilize an electronic repair order system or a parts inventory control system must generate a monthly report which tracks all activity for parts returned to inventory. A copy of this adjustments to inventory report should be generated prior to the monthly close and retained in the Parts Department. Record Retention & Administration Requirements 149
exclusions: Repairs performed using non-genuine Hyundai parts without prior approval from the District Parts and Service Manager.
Hyundai-branded parts not supplied by HMA and intended for use outside the U.S. are considered non-genuine for warranty repairs unless approved.
dealer_actions: Provide documentation to substantiate that a specific part stated on the warranty claim was used to repair the vehicle.
The repair order must indicate and include the parts charged out to the repair order for a specific vehicle.

For Dealers using an automated parts inventory system, a numbered counter requisition cross-referenced with the repair order or point of sale document and a date of transfer of parts from inventory, must be completed each time parts are removed from inventory.
Each counter requisition number must be recorded on the repair order or the point of sale document.
Every part removed from inventory must be listed on a counter requisition.
Counter requisitions must be accounted for and filed in numeric sequence.

All invoices or copies covering parts or services purchased from vendors or other shops (and a record of payment of such invoices), new car invoices and any other records used by the Dealer to substantiate the information recorded on the claim, must be retained by the Dealer for a period of five (5) years from date of claim payment.

For repairs performed using other than Genuine Hyundai Parts purchased from HMA, the non-genuine Hyundai part supplier’s name, invoice number, and part number must be recorded on all copies of the repair order including the customer’s receipt copy.
The supplier’s invoice for non-genuine parts must be retained with other accounting files.
Obtain prior approval from the District Parts and Service Manager for the use of non-genuine Hyundai parts for warranty repairs.
In the event that a requisitioned part is not utilized in a repair and returned to inventory, this fact must be documented.
Dealers who utilize an electronic repair order system or a parts inventory control system must generate a monthly report which tracks all activity for parts returned to inventory.
A copy of the monthly adjustments to inventory report should be generated prior to the monthly close and retained in the Parts Department.

title: Sublet Repair Claiming and Documentation Requirements
raw_content: When sublet repairs are performed, the Dealer’s repair order must contain the following sublet final invoice cross-reference information: • Description of sublet repair • Name of sublet vendor repair shop • Date of sublet repair • Purchase order number issued for sublet repair • Sublet repair invoice number • Sublet amount The sublet repair final invoice must be itemized and contain the following repair order cross- reference information: • Dealer’s repair order number • Vehicle Identification Number (VIN) or vehicle license plate number • Date of sublet repair • Mileage of vehicle • Mileage vehicle towed (if applicable) • Purchase order number issued for sublet repair • Sublet amount • Reasons and details of repair performed • Detailed breakdown of part(s) and labor: • Detailed part(s) itemized • Detailed labor itemized The original copy of the sublet vendor final invoice must be attached to the accounting copy of the repair order. A copy of the sublet vendor final invoice must be attached to the technician input/shop hard copy of the repair order which is filed in the service files. All sublets require an final invoice as well as any other pertinent information to be attached. If the Dealer supplies the sublet vendor with the part(s) required to perform the sublet repair, the Dealer is to claim the part(s) on the warranty claim as a detail part(s) (enter part under Part Information section on WebDCS). The Dealer supplied part(s) is not to be shown or claimed on the vendor’s sublet repair final invoice. If the vendor supplies the part(s), the vendor supplied part(s) cost is to be included on the vendor’s sublet repair final invoice amount. The amount of sublet requested on the claim for reimbursement must be the actual net sublet cost incurred by the Dealership. All sublet amounts claimed must be the actual sublet amount cost paid to the sublet vendor, less any discounts or rebates. Note: Sublet amount over $100 will require Additional Comments to be entered in the warranty claim. Record Retention & Administration Requirements 150
coverage_details: Reimbursement for actual net sublet repair costs incurred by the dealership.
Reimbursement for detailed parts supplied by the dealer, claimed separately on the warranty claim.
Reimbursement for vendor-supplied parts when their cost is included in the vendor's sublet invoice amount.
exclusions: Dealer-supplied parts must not be shown or claimed on the sublet vendor’s final invoice.
Sublet amounts claimed that are not the actual net cost paid to the vendor.
Sublet amounts claimed that do not reflect deductions for any discounts or rebates.
dealer_actions: Ensure the Dealer’s repair order contains: description of sublet repair, name of sublet vendor repair shop, date of sublet repair, purchase order number issued for sublet repair, sublet repair invoice number, and sublet amount.

Ensure the sublet repair final invoice is itemized and contains: Dealer’s repair order number, Vehicle Identification Number (VIN) or vehicle license plate number, date of sublet repair, vehicle mileage, mileage vehicle towed (if applicable), purchase order number, sublet amount, reasons and details of repair performed, and a detailed breakdown of part(s) and labor.
Attach the original copy of the sublet vendor final invoice to the accounting copy of the repair order.
Attach a copy of the sublet vendor final invoice to the technician input/shop hard copy of the repair order, filed in service files.
Attach all pertinent information, including the final invoice, for all sublets.
If the Dealer supplies parts for a sublet repair, claim the part(s) on the warranty claim as detail part(s) under the Part Information section on WebDCS.
Ensure that if the vendor supplies parts, the vendor-supplied part(s) cost is included on the vendor’s sublet repair final invoice amount.
Claim the actual net sublet cost incurred by the Dealership for reimbursement.
Claim the actual sublet amount cost paid to the sublet vendor, less any discounts or rebates.
Enter 'Additional Comments' in the warranty claim for any sublet amount exceeding $100.

title: Record Retention & Administration Requirements
raw_content: Service management is responsible for verifying that the repair order contains accurate in/out mileage, open/ close dates, owner-described symptoms and clear legible instructions, before issuing the technician input/ shop hard copy of the repair order to the technician. Service management is also responsible for: • Identifying on the technician input/shop hard copy of the repair order the specific employee name or number (supervision/salary/hourly) assigned to perform the warranty/customer satisfaction repair. • Verifying that the Service Advisor enters the nature of the customer’s complaint. • Verifying that the repairing technician enters the cause of the customer’s complaint and enters a description of the repair on the repair order technician input/shop hard copy (3 Cs). • Verifying that the correct nature and cause codes are entered on the repair order processing copy and warranty claim. • Entering on the repair order any additional repairs that are required but not discovered until after the repair order initial write-up. The additional repair entries must be signed by the Service Manager, next to each actual repair, to indicate approval of the additional repairs before the repairs are performed. (This signature is required even if the additional repairs are written-in by service management). • Signing starting “on” and completion “off” clocking times for actual straight time repairs. • Signing any hand written time recording entries. Record Retention & Administration Requirements 151
coverage_details: Service management responsibilities for accurate repair order processing and warranty/customer satisfaction repair documentation.
dealer_actions: Verify that the repair order contains accurate in/out mileage, open/close dates, owner-described symptoms, and clear legible instructions before issuing the technician input/shop hard copy to the technician.
Identify on the technician input/shop hard copy of the repair order the specific employee name or number (supervision/salary/hourly) assigned to perform the warranty/customer satisfaction repair.
Verify that the Service Advisor enters the nature of the customer’s complaint.
Verify that the repairing technician enters the cause of the customer’s complaint and enters a description of the repair on the repair order technician input/shop hard copy (3 Cs).
Verify that the correct nature and cause codes are entered on the repair order processing copy and warranty claim.
Enter on the repair order any additional repairs that are required but not discovered until after the repair order initial write-up.

Ensure that additional repair entries are signed by the Service Manager, next to each actual repair, to indicate approval before the repairs are performed (signature required even if written-in by service management).
Sign starting “on” and completion “off” clocking times for actual straight time repairs.
Sign any handwritten time recording entries.

title: Warranty Administration & Record Keeping
raw_content: To administer the warranties, the Dealer must establish and maintain a customer/VIN service file. Maintaining appropriate records and supporting documents will enable the Dealer to service the owner better and minimize the possibility of comebacks and denied claims or charge-backs due to servicing ineligible vehicles. Files must be filed/organized by VIN.
exclusions: Denied claims or charge-backs for servicing ineligible vehicles.
dealer_actions: Establish and maintain a customer/VIN service file.
Maintain appropriate records and supporting documents.
Organize files by VIN.

title: Record Retention & Administration Requirements
raw_content: • Indicate the vehicle identification number (VIN) on the file folder tab for each new vehicle received. • Arrange folders in numerical sequence by the complete 17 digit VIN, the last six digits of the VIN, or the short VIN which is comprised of the 6th, 10th, and 12th through 17th digits of the complete 17 digit VIN. • Insert copies of all repair orders, signed Pre-Delivery Inspection forms, claims, Warranty Vehicle Information screen printouts, and other required documents in the corresponding folders by date as prepared. • Write the owner information and date of sale or date of first use on the folder tab as soon as available. The Vehicle Identification Number filing system allows for a file to be started upon completion of a new vehicle Pre-Delivery Inspection. This type of filing also allows for new and/or multiple owners, thereby better recognizing comeback repairs and vehicle service history. Record Retention & Administration Requirements 152
dealer_actions: Indicate the vehicle identification number (VIN) on the file folder tab for each new vehicle received
Arrange folders in numerical sequence by the complete 17 digit VIN, the last six digits of the VIN, or the short VIN (6th, 10th, and 12th-17th digits of the complete 17 digit VIN)
Insert copies of all repair orders, signed Pre-Delivery Inspection forms, claims, Warranty Vehicle Information screen printouts, and other required documents in corresponding folders by date
Write the owner information and date of sale or date of first use on the folder tab as soon as available
Start a vehicle file upon completion of a new vehicle Pre-Delivery Inspection

title: Record Retention & Administration Requirements
raw_content: HMA may conduct audits of Dealer claims and claim supporting records in accordance with provisions of the Dealer Sales and Service Agreement (refer to Section 1). Records supporting wholesale parts purchases or parts incentives may also be examined during the audit. An audit does not imply that improper practices are being or have been engaged in, however, a chargeback will be made if the audit reveals that improper or unsubstantiated claims have been submitted by the Dealer and paid by HMA. HMA may also conduct audits of Dealer’s compliance of calibration and operation of shop tools and equipment in accordance with Manufacturer’s required calibration schedule. Improper and/or unsubstantiated claims include, but are not limited to, those claims involving: • Unauthorized replacement of assemblies or parts. • Misrepresentation of parts acquisition cost or sourcing of part. • Unauthorized use of non-Hyundai Genuine Parts. • Parts claimed at a rate in excess of parts purchased. • Parts received from Dealer Parts Department inventory prior to performance of repair. • Defective parts not returned to Parts Department by technician. • Special ordered parts not documented or indicated. • Incorrect labor operation. • Unauthorized add-on, repeat, duplicate, comeback or improper repairs or over repairs. • Unnecessary repairs performed which are not based on a customer complaint or repairs where no corrective action is taken, except as authorized by an HMA representative. • Customer signed Early Bird envelope without an attached RO hard copy. Early Bird envelope does not replace RO requirements. • Printed (or digitally captured using tablet) pre-workorders used for customer write-ups not attached to an RO hardcopy. Properly completed pre-workorders must be retained and attached to the RO hardcopy. • Printed (or digitally captured using tablet) Multi-Point Inspection (MPI) sheets not attached to an RO hardcopy. Properly completed MPI sheet(s) must be retained and attached to the RO hardcopy. • No customer signatures authorizing repair(s) or acknowledgement of repair completion. • Reprinted lost or damaged repair orders without signed authorization from the Service Manager. • Signed reprint without the original repair order. When utilizing reprints, the original repair order with customer signature at the time of original write up must be retained. • No technician remarks or incomplete technician remarks, including required before and after measurements/readings. Record Retention & Administration Requirements 153 • Unsubstantiated labor time. • No supporting documentation or inadequate supporting documentation. • Reconditioning of used vehicle inventory at HMA Warranty expense. • Charging HMA for shop materials. • Repairs not covered by warranty or campaign, repairs related to improper installation of an accessory, repairs related to an accident, to customer modification, neglect, abuse, or lack of maintenance, or any claims which are not Hyundai responsibility. • Misrepresented VIN, in-service date, repair date, mileage, causal part, labor operation, sublet, freight, or towing. • Misrepresented HMA authorization, deliberate claim splitting to avoid authorization or claims where payment received exceeds the amount authorized. • Duplicate submission and payment for a repair. • Related concerns for same repair on separate claims. • Work not performed as claimed. • Improper actual time recording to substantiate repair. • Parts not available for inspection, improperly completed Warranty Tag, not defective as alleged, or not applicable to model, body style or trim level. • Technician concurrently working on two or more vehicles or repair orders at one time. • Payroll records which do not support technician payment or amount of payment to technician. • Unauthorized mark-up of sublet, NPN parts, towing or freight. • Any claim in which repairs and/or record retention policy and procedures were not properly adhered to. • Any claim in which repairs and/or record retention policy and procedures were not properly adhered to. The list of examples shown above is not all-inclusive. The dealer has the responsibility to fully adhere to the Warranty Policy and Procedures provisions, including verifying that repairs are eligible and needed due to a warrantable defect, regardless of: • Prior Approval (PA) • On-site inspector approval • Digital imaging approval • Techline, Field Service Engineer involvement, etc. Company involvement, including prior repair approvals, does not exempt the repair from warranty audits, review, or chargeback. Record Retention & Administration Requirements 154
coverage_details: HMA may conduct audits of Dealer claims and claim supporting records in accordance with provisions of the Dealer Sales and Service Agreement.
HMA may examine records supporting wholesale parts purchases or parts incentives during audits.
HMA may conduct audits of Dealer’s compliance of calibration and operation of shop tools and equipment in accordance with Manufacturer’s required calibration schedule.
exclusions: Claims involving unauthorized replacement of assemblies or parts.
Claims involving misrepresentation of parts acquisition cost or sourcing of parts.
Claims involving unauthorized use of non-Hyundai Genuine Parts.
Claims for parts at a rate in excess of parts purchased.
Claims for parts received from Dealer Parts Department inventory prior to performance of repair.
Claims where defective parts were not returned to the Parts Department by the technician.
Claims for special ordered parts not documented or indicated.
Claims with incorrect labor operations.
Claims involving unauthorized add-on, repeat, duplicate, comeback, improper repairs, or over repairs.
Claims for unnecessary repairs performed which are not based on a customer complaint or where no corrective action is taken, unless authorized by an HMA representative.
Claims with a customer signed Early Bird envelope without an attached Repair Order (RO) hard copy.
Claims with printed or digitally captured pre-workorders used for customer write-ups not attached to an RO hardcopy.
Claims with printed or digitally captured Multi-Point Inspection (MPI) sheets not attached to an RO hardcopy.
Claims lacking customer signatures authorizing repair(s) or acknowledgement of repair completion.
Claims with reprinted lost or damaged repair orders without signed authorization from the Service Manager.
Claims with a signed reprint without the original repair order (when utilizing reprints, the original RO with customer signature at the time of original write-up must be retained).
Claims lacking technician remarks or having incomplete technician remarks, including required before and after measurements/readings.
Claims with unsubstantiated labor time.
Claims lacking supporting documentation or having inadequate supporting documentation.
Claims for reconditioning of used vehicle inventory at HMA Warranty expense.
Claims charging HMA for shop materials.

Claims for repairs not covered by warranty or campaign, repairs related to improper installation of an accessory, repairs related to an accident, to customer modification, neglect, abuse, or lack of maintenance, or any claims which are not Hyundai responsibility.
Claims with misrepresented VIN, in-service date, repair date, mileage, causal part, labor operation, sublet, freight, or towing.
Claims with misrepresented HMA authorization, deliberate claim splitting to avoid authorization, or claims where payment received exceeds the amount authorized.
Duplicate submission and payment for a repair.
Related concerns for the same repair on separate claims.
Claims for work not performed as claimed.
Claims with improper actual time recording to substantiate repair.
Claims where parts are not available for inspection, Warranty Tag is improperly completed, parts are not defective as alleged, or not applicable to model, body style or trim level.
Claims where a technician concurrently worked on two or more vehicles or repair orders at one time.
Claims where payroll records do not support technician payment or amount of payment to technician.
Claims with unauthorized mark-up of sublet, Non-Parts Numbered (NPN) parts, towing, or freight.
Any claim in which repairs and/or record retention policy and procedures were not properly adhered to.

Company involvement, including prior repair approvals (PA, on-site inspector approval, digital imaging approval, Techline, Field Service Engineer involvement), does not exempt the repair from warranty audits, review, or chargeback.
dealer_actions: Retain claims and claim supporting records for audit.
Retain records supporting wholesale parts purchases or parts incentives for examination during audits.
Ensure compliance with Manufacturer’s required calibration schedule for shop tools and equipment.
Avoid submitting improper or unsubstantiated claims to prevent chargebacks.
Ensure proper authorization for replacement of assemblies or parts.
Accurately represent parts acquisition cost and sourcing.
Use only Hyundai Genuine Parts for warranty repairs unless explicitly authorized otherwise.
Claim parts at a rate not in excess of parts purchased.
Ensure parts claimed were received from Dealer Parts Department inventory after performance of repair.
Return defective parts to the Parts Department by the technician.
Properly document or indicate special ordered parts.
Use correct labor operations.
Avoid unauthorized add-on, repeat, duplicate, comeback, improper repairs, or over repairs.
Perform repairs based on customer complaints or with explicit authorization from an HMA representative.
Attach the Repair Order (RO) hard copy to any customer signed Early Bird envelope.
Retain and attach properly completed pre-workorders to the RO hardcopy.
Retain and attach properly completed Multi-Point Inspection (MPI) sheet(s) to the RO hardcopy.
Obtain customer signatures authorizing repair(s) and acknowledgement of repair completion.
Obtain signed authorization from the Service Manager for reprinted lost or damaged repair orders.
When utilizing reprints, retain the original repair order with customer signature at the time of original write up.
Provide complete technician remarks, including required before and after measurements/readings.
Provide adequate supporting documentation for all claims.
Do not recondition used vehicle inventory at HMA Warranty expense.

Only submit claims for repairs covered by warranty or campaign, or which are Hyundai responsibility (not for improper accessory installation, accidents, customer modification, neglect, abuse, or lack of maintenance).
Accurately represent VIN, in-service date, repair date, mileage, causal part, labor operation, sublet, freight, and towing.
Accurately represent HMA authorization, avoid deliberate claim splitting, and ensure payment requested does not exceed the authorized amount.
Avoid duplicate submission and payment for a repair.
Consolidate related concerns for the same repair on a single claim.
Ensure work is performed as claimed.
Properly record actual time to substantiate repair.
Ensure parts are available for inspection, Warranty Tags are properly completed, parts are defective as alleged, and are applicable to the correct model, body style, or trim level.
Ensure technicians are not concurrently working on two or more vehicles or repair orders when claiming time.
Maintain payroll records that support technician payment and amount of payment for claimed work.
Avoid unauthorized mark-up of sublet, NPN parts, towing, or freight.
Fully adhere to the Warranty Policy and Procedures provisions for all claims and record retention.
